entry.title: Implementing Event-Driven Architecture with Canonical Data Model in SAP CPI
entry.content:
Event-Driven Architecture (EDA):
Event-Driven Architecture is a design pattern in which system components communicate by producing and consuming events. An event represents a change of state (e.g., a new sales order, product update). In EDA, producers send events without knowing who consumes them, enabling loose coupling between systems.

Key benefits:

  • Decoupling

  • Real-time data flow

  • Scalability and flexibility

Canonical Data Model (CDM):
A Canonical Data Model is a standardized format for data exchange between systems. Instead of each system mapping data differently, a single unified model is used across all integrations.

The Integration Flow implementation looks like this:

  • Step 1: Prepare Canonical Data

  • Prepare your canonical XML/JSON payload. Here’s a sample XML code:

     

<Root>
	<Products>
		<Product>
			<Price>2.5</Price>
			<Rating>4</Rating>
			<DiscontinuedDate/>
			<ID>0</ID>
			<ReleaseDate>1992-01-01T00:00:00.000</ReleaseDate>
		</Product>
	</Products>
	<Categories>
		<Category>
			<ID>0</ID>
			<Name>Food</Name>
		</Category>
	</Categories>
	<Suppliers>
		<Supplier>
			<Address>
				<State>WA</State>
				<ZipCode>98074</ZipCode>
				<Street>NE 228th</Street>
				<Country>USA</Country>
				<City>Sammamish</City>
			</Address>
			<Concurrency>0</Concurrency>
			<ID>0</ID>
			<Name>Exotic Liquids</Name>
		</Supplier>
	</Suppliers>
	<Employee>
		<Name>Hirva</Name>
		<ID>4</ID>
	</Employee>
	<Book>
		<Book_ID>101</Book_ID>
		<Book_Title>Effective Java</Book_Title>
		<Book_Author>Joshua Bloch</Book_Author>
		<Book_Description>A comprehensive guide to best practices in Java programming.</Book_Description>
	</Book>
	<Customers>
		<Customer>
			<Number>1</Number>
			<FirstName>Fred</FirstName>
			<LastName>Landis</LastName>
			<Address>
				<Street>Oakstreet</Street>
				<City>Boston</City>
				<ZIP>23320</ZIP>
				<State>MA</State>
			</Address>
		</Customer>
	</Customers>
	<breakfast_menu>
		<food>
			<name>Belgian Waffles</name>
			<price>$5.95</price>
			<description>Two of our famous Belgian Waffles with plenty of real maple syrup</description>
			<calories>650</calories>
		</food>
	</breakfast_menu>
</Root>

Step 2: Send Data from Postman

  • Method: POST

  • URL: Your SAP CPI iFlow endpoint (e.g., https://<tenant>.iflmap.sap.hana.ondemand.com/http/canonicalEvent)

  • Header: Content-Type: application/xml

    Body: Paste the sample canonical message that in the step-1 given.

    Step 3: Build SAP CPI iFlow

    Component Purpose

    HTTPS Sender AdapterReceives HTTP POST from Postman
    Content Modifier (optional)Logs or adds initial data
    Router (Content-Based)Routes based on Type (SalesOrder, Product)
    JMS Receiver AdapterSends message to the target JMS queue (SalesOrderQueue, ProductQueue)
    parallel multicastAllows you to send the same message to multiple routes for parallel processing

    Router Conditions:

    If using XML XPath:

    • Condition 1 (Sales Order):
      //CanonicalMessage/Type = 'SalesOrder'

    • Condition 2 (Product):
      //CanonicalMessage/Type = 'Product'

      Step 4: Configure JMS Queues

      1.In SAP CPI Cockpit:

      • Go to JMS Resources

      • Create Queues:

        • SalesOrderQueue

        • ProductQueue

      2.In CPI iFlow:

      • Add JMS Receiver Adapter

      • Point each route to its respective queue.

      Step 5: Deploy & Test

      1.Deploy the iFlow.
      2.Post Data using Postman.
      3.Check Monitoring:

      • In SAP CPI Message Monitor, confirm successful routing.

      • In JMS Queue Monitor, confirm that the message lands in the correct queue.

      Error Handling: Sending Email on Exception


      Steps to Implement Email Notification in Error Handling:

      1. Add Exception Subprocess:
      In your main iFlow:

      • Drag and drop an Exception Subprocess (gray dotted box around your main flow).

      • This subprocess will automatically catch any unhandled exceptions in the main flow

        2.Add Error Handling Components:

         

      •  

        Component Purpose

        Start Event (Escalation)Starts the exception subprocess
        Content ModifierPrepares email content (subject, body, etc.)
        Mail Receiver AdapterSends the actual email

        3.Configure the Content Modifier (Prepare Email):

        Exchange Property:

        • EmailSubject: Error in CPI iFlow: Canonical Event Router

        • EmailBody: {As like you want}

          4. Configure Mail Receiver Adapter:

        • Protocol: Mail

        • SMTP Server: Your mail server (e.g., smtp.office365.com)

        • Authentication: Yes (configure in Security Materials)

        • To: e.g., support@yourcompany.com

        • Subject: ${property.EmailSubject}

        • Body: ${property.EmailBody}

        Make sure your CPI tenant has Mail (SMTP) connectivity set up.

        5. Deploy & Test:

        • Force an error: You can test by deliberately sending invalid data from Postman.

        • Check:

          • CPI Monitoring (Exception Subprocess should trigger)

          • Email inbox (verify error notification received) if any error occurs.

            The detailed error mail triggers like this if any error happens in the entire workflow and i used the HTML code in the mail triggering body to make email looks even more beautiful.

          • If Error doesn't occurs the data would goes to their respective JMS Queues.

entry.title: SAP SOAP Sender & Receiver Adapters: Your Integration Game Changers
entry.content:
SOAP Sender and Receiver Adapters in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI)

Ever felt lost in the maze of SAP integration options? You’re not alone! I’m Rishika Sairi, an SAP Integration Specialist passionate about making complex topics simple. In this blog, I’ll show you how to use the SOAP Sender Adapter in SAP CPI to connect inbound SOAP requests with an OData receiver—step by step and hands-on.

If you find this guide helpful, drop a comment or give a thumbs up—your kudos keep me motivated to share more!

 

 Use Case Overview

We want to expose a SOAP service that:

  1. Accepts an order number via a SOAP request.
  2. Fetches the shipment date using an OData service.
  3. Responds back with the enriched order response.

Why use SOAP instead of REST, OData, or GraphQL?

  • SOAP is chosen for strict data contracts, strong security, and reliable messaging—ideal for enterprise and SAP integration. It uses XML and works well for complex operations and when both sender and receiver must agree on message formats. REST and GraphQL are more flexible and modern, but SOAP’s structure is better for scenarios needing formal agreements and type safety

WSDL and Its Role in SOAP

  • WSDL (Web Services Description Language) is an XML file that describes what a SOAP service can do: available operations, input/output formats, and where to access it.
  • The WSDL acts as a contract. Both sender and receiver use it to generate code and ensure messages match exactly, reducing errors and making integration predictable.

 

Summary Table

Feature

SOAP (with WSDL)

REST/OData/GraphQL

Contract

Strict (WSDL)

Flexible/Schema-based

Security

Built-in (WS-Security)

Custom/simple

Data Format

XML

JSON/XML

Complexity

Higher

Lower

 

SOAP + WSDL = strong contract, type safety, and predictable integration—best for SAP scenarios needing reliability and clear structure

Step-by-Step iFlow Design

Step 1. Start a New Integration Flow Project

  • Open SAP Integration Suite → Design → Create an Integration Flow.
  • Name: SOAP_sender_Adapter.

Step 2. Configure the SOAP Sender Adapter

  • Drag the SOAP sender from the Sender section.

 

 

Field

Value used

Explanation / What You Need to Do

Address*

/SoapUIdemo

The endpoint path for the SOAP sender. Clients will call the service at https://<tenant_url>/cxf/SoapUIdemo.

Service Definition

WSDL

Indicates the service is defined via a WSDL file.

Use WS-Addressing

Unchecked

Leave unchecked unless the client requires WS-Addressing.

URL to WSDL*

/wsdl/GetOrderShipmentDetails.wsdl

Relative path to the uploaded WSDL file within the CPI tenant. Make sure this WSDL is uploaded under the "Resources" section of the iFlow.

Service

p2:GetOrderShipDetailsService

The service name defined in the WSDL (<wsdl:service> tag). Must match the WSDL exactly.

Endpoint

p2:GetOrderShipDetailsServiceSoap

The port/endpoint name from the WSDL (<wsdl:port> inside the <service>). Also must match exactly.

Processing Settings

Robust

Recommended setting for reliable SOAP communication. Ensures message persistence and retries in case of failure.

Authorization

User Role

Indicates that clients must authenticate using a role-based access policy.

User Role

ESBMessaging.send

CPI's default role for sending messages. The calling user (e.g., a technical user) must be assigned this role in the SAP BTP cockpit.

 

What You Need to Configure

  1. WSDL File Upload
    • Go to your iFlow → Resources → WSDL.
    • Upload the file named GetOrderShipmentDetails.wsdl.
    • Get the wsdl file from this drive [ https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/14OdkKwHEkMeH_Jdfvamj4LaAWvgNlP9x?usp=sharing ] 
    • Ensure the file path matches /wsdl/GetOrderShipmentDetails.wsdl.
  2. Correct Service and Endpoint Names
    • <wsdl:service name="GetOrderShipDetailsService">
    • <wsdl:port name="GetOrderShipDetailsServiceSoap">
    • Open the WSDL and verify:
    • The namespace prefix p2: should also match the targetNamespace in the WSDL.
  3. Security Role Assignment
    • Assign the role ESBMessaging.send to the user/service instance that will invoke this SOAP service.
    • Use Basic Authentication or OAuth as required.
    • In SAP BTP Cockpit:
  4. Endpoint URL for Clients
    • Your external client can access the service via: https://<your-cpi-tenant>/cxf/SoapUIdemo
    • Make sure this is reachable from the client environment (firewall, IP allowlist, etc.).

 

 

Step 3. Add a Content Modifier to extract and store the Order Number from the SOAP payload.

 

Field

Value

Explanation

Action

Create

Creates a new message header with the name specified. If the header already exists, it will overwrite it.

Name

OrderNumber

Name of the message header being created. It will be available throughout the iFlow.

Source Type

XPath

Extracts the value using an XPath expression from the incoming XML payload.

Source Value

//OrderNumber

The XPath expression pointing to the OrderNumber element in the request payload.

Data Type

java.lang.String

The expected data type of the header value.

 

 

Step 4: Using OData

  • Add a Request-Reply step to call the OData backend.
  • Drag and connect an OData Adapter to a Receiver.

 

Why Am I using Northwind Service:

I chose the Northwind service because it offers ready-to-use sample order data with no authentication required, follows standard OData conventions for easy querying, and is well-documented, making it perfect for quick testing and integration scenarios

 

Field

Value

Explanation

Address

https://services.odata.org/NorthWind/Northwind.svc

The base URL of the OData service endpoint.

Proxy Type

Internet

Connects over the internet. Choose "On-Premise" if using a Cloud Connector.

Authentication

None

This public service does not require authentication. Use "Basic" or "OAuth2" if needed.

CSRF Protected

Unchecked

Not needed for GET requests; CSRF is typically used with POST, PUT, DELETE.

Reuse Connection

Checked

Improves performance by reusing HTTP connections.

 

 

Field

Value

Explanation

Operation Details

Query (GET)

Fetch data using a GET request.

Resource Path

Orders

Target entity set from the OData service.

Query Options

$select=OrderID,CustomerID,...

Only selected fields are fetched for better performance. Complete list: OrderID, CustomerID, EmployeeID, OrderDate, RequiredDate, ShippedDate, ShipVia, Freight, ShipName, ShipAddress, ShipCity, ShipRegion, ShipPostalCode, ShipCountry.

Enable Batch Processing

Unchecked

Not needed for single queries. Enable for multiple operations.

Custom Query Options

Empty

Can be used for filters or $expand if needed.

Content Type

Atom

XML-based format. Change to JSON if preferred and supported by target service.

Page Size

Empty

Optional. Used for pagination in large result sets.

 

 

  • The Connection Source has two options, one is Remote and the other one is Local EDMX file. As the NorthWindService doesn’t have any kind of authentication to clear I have used up the Remote one.
  • The operation is set to Query (GET) for the order details
  • Entity – Orders, we must select the fields what we needed according to requirement. I have selected all the fields.

 

 

 

  • Verify the Query after selecting fields, If any changes need to be in the query we must modify in the step 3.
  • Click Finish.

 

Step 5: Mapping the Response

  • Add a Message Mapping step to map the OData response to the final SOAP response structure.
  • Input: OData format.
  • Output: SOAP structure.

Why Is Mapping Needed?

  1. OData responses are typically in JSON or XML with a different format than the SOAP response.
  2. Mapping helps reshape this data into the required SOAP structure.
  • After the SOAP request is received, the OData call fetches additional data (e.g., shipment date) related to the order.
  • The final SOAP response must follow a specific schema or structure defined by the SOAP service (WSDL/XSD).

NOTE: Here in the mapping the 1:1 mapping are always mandatory. I have taken the reference of the names from both sides and matched them accordingly.

Step 6: Add End Event

  • Place an End Message after the mapping to complete the flow.

Step 7: Deploy the Integration flow and Note the URL (Needs to send to the receiver).

SOAP API EXECUTION USING SOAP UI:

  • You can download the Soap UI from here https://www.soapui.org/
  • Create a new soap project and import the necessary WSDL File 

 

 

 

 

  • Place the SOAP API URL in the designated place
  • Set the Auth to basic and give me the SOAP API tenants Integration flow service key credentials
  • Send the request by tapping the play symbol along with filling the OrderNumber to be 10250[My reference].

SOAP Receiver Adapter

In this, we'll walk through designing an iFlow where a client sends an HTTPS request (in JSON), and the integration flow:

  • Converts it to XML,
  • Maps the data,
  • Sends it to a SOAP backend via SOAP Receiver Adapter,
  • And returns the response.

RESONS FOR SECOND IFLOW

  • We are consuming the SOAP Service that is in the first integration flow like exploring the SOAP Receiver Adapter
  • Instead of using the SOAP UI for sending the request we are using the REST API thorough HTTP to consume the Soap API Service.

 Use Case Overview

An external application (HTTPS client) sends a JSON request. The iFlow:

  1. Converts JSON to XML.
  2. Maps the message to match the SOAP backend schema.
  3. Sends the message to a backend system using the SOAP receiver.
  4. Returns the backend response to the client.

Step-by-Step iFlow Design

Step1: Create a New Integration Flow

  • Go to SAP Integration Suite → Design → Create iFlow.
  • Name: Soap_Receiver_Adapter.

 

Step 2: Configure HTTPS Sender

  • Add an HTTPS sender adapter.
  • Configure the Address (e.g., /Receiveradapterr).
  • Authorization- UserRole>ESBMessaging.send

Step 3: Convert JSON to XML

  • Use a JSON to XML Converter step.
  • This is required because SOAP backends typically consume XML, not JSON.  

 

  1. Message Mapping
  • Add a Message Mapping step to convert incoming XML structure to the format expected by the SOAP backend.

 

Source: Accepts XSD. Convert the xml payload that you have sent in postman.

Simulation:

  • The sample payload in this case is
    <?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8'?><root><productIdentifier>HT-2000</productIdentifier></root>
  • This is the XML file that is used after adding the root element to the payload that you are sending through the postman

 

Connection Details:

  • Address: The service endpoint URL is: https://5b61c35atrial-it-cpitrial05-rt.cfapps.us10-001.hana.ondemand.com/cxf/SoapUIdemo
    This is the actual URL where the SOAP request will be sent(from the sender).
  • Proxy Type:
    Set to Internet, which means the connection is routed through the CPI internet proxy.
  • URL to WSDL:
    /wsdl/GetOrderShipmentDetails.wsdl
    This is the relative path to the WSDL file used to define the SOAP service.
  • Service, Endpoint, and Operation Name:
    These are parsed from the WSDL:
    • Service: p1:GetOrderShipDetailsService
    • Endpoint: p1:GetOrderShipDetailsServiceSoap
    • Operation Name: p1:GetOrderShipmentDetails
      This operation will be triggered when the message is sent.
  • Authentication:
    Set to Basic with the Credential Name as Servicekey.
    CPI will look up a credential entry with this name (maintained in the Security Material) and use its username/password.
  • Timeout (in ms):
    Set to 60000, meaning the integration flow will wait up to 60 seconds for a response.
  • Keep-Alive:
    Enabled. This allows the connection to be reused, improving performance on multiple requests.

WS-Security Configuration (SOAP Receiver Adapter)

  1. None – No WS-Security headers added. Use when not required by the service.
  2. Based on Policies in WSDL – Auto-applies security from WSDL-defined policies.
  3. Via Manual Configuration in Channel – Add custom WS-Security headers manually (e.g., UsernameToken, Signature).

Use based on target service’s security needs.

Deploy the Integration Flow and monitor it in the Trace mode.

Trigger the HTTP endpoint with the appropriate payload using postman and generate token using service key from integration flow instance.

Disclaimer:
All the views and opinions in this blog are my own and are made in my personal capacity. SAP shall not be responsible or liable for any of the contents published in this blog.

entry.title: Integration Assessment - Manage Request Workflow with APIs
entry.content:
Introduction

Integration Assessment, a capability of SAP Integration Suite, allows you to evaluate and maintain consistent use of integration technologies across your integration landscape. It is based on the Integration Solution Advisory Methodology (ISAM) and utilizes a workflow-based based approach to gather requirements and choose the appropriate integration technology for specific integration needs.

Previously, the workflow was entirely UI-driven, but we now offer specific APIs to facilitate better integration with external systems.

The sections below will guide you on how to utilize the APIs to integrate the process with external systems. It will provide examples, which will help you to use the APIs for yourself.

Preconditions

To follow the examples, the following preconditions must be fulfilled:

  • You have a valid SAP Integration Suite subscription and activated the Integration Assessment capability.
  • You enabled the API access to Integration assessment and created a service key.
  • You are able to access the API via a REST client (e.g. Postman).
  • You are familiar with the Integration Assessment Request Workflow, i.e. the concept of Business Solution Requests and Interface Requests including their technical entities (Request, RequestLineItem, IntegrationFlow, MessageFlow)

The examples given in the article point to the API endpoint of the EU10 landscape (https://intas-api.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/intas/entities/v1). The specific endpoint you need to use will be visible in the service key of the related Integration Assessment API service instance.

For accessing the UI, this article assumes the subdomain 'thisisanexample', resulting in the URL https://thisisanexample.intas.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com

Workflow

The example in this article will demonstrate how to create and process business solution requests and interface request using the API. The primary concept is to generate all the request entities via APIs while handling the requirement analysis and technology selection via the UI. This way, external systems can initiate the workflow, while the more complex technology selection is directly offered by the service. At the end of the technology selection, users can download a PDF summary of the selections made and process this further in an external system.

Create Business Solution Request

The first step to initially start the workflow is to create the business solution request. This can be easily achieved via the API.

POST on https://intas-api.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/intas/entities/v1/Request with body

{
  "Name": "New Business Solution Request"
}

see full specification here

This call will create a new business solution request and return the newly created entity. It is important to note down the id property as we need this for upcoming calls. We assume the id value '8deb4b5c-0679-4048-ad9c-fd4956a0025c'.

The business solution request is represented by the technical entity Request. If it is created initially, it will be created in draft status. You can see the possible states a Request can have in the API description here.

Answer Business Solution Request Questionnaire

For further processing the questionnaire for the request needs to be answered. You can do that by accessing following URL: https://thisisanexample.intas.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/app/index.html#/Worklist/SolutionRequest/8deb4b5c-0679-4048-ad9c-fd4956a0025c

Note that the GUID attached at the end of the URL is the one from the previously created request.

This step may be skipped in case you define an empty questionnaire for the Business Solution Request

Submitting the request and adding an Interface Request is not necessary as this is done via API in the next step.

Create Interface Request

A new Interface Request can be created by using the API

POST on https://intas-api.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/intas/entities/v1/CreateInterfaceRequest

{
	"Name": "New Interface Request",
	"RequestId": "8deb4b5c-0679-4048-ad9c-fd4956a0025c",
	"StyleId": "15562407-5770-45ea-aa26-b9e6cd36ab12",
	"SourceApplicationInstances": [
		{"Id":  "42b4a6f6-310f-40b2-bcd8-625fbce558dd"}
	],
	"TargetApplicationInstances": [
		{"Id": "46b37842-b7fa-404f-a5b7-d919c6400dc2"},
		{"Id": "c4be1e2b-a581-4759-b7bb-12e639f2452a"}
	]
}

see full specification here

Beside a name for the interface request you also need to provide additional parameters:

  • RequestId: The id of the previously generated request.
  • StyleId: The id of the style to use. In this case the id refers to the style "Process Integration", which is part of the standard content delivered. You can get the ids of the styles in your system by using the respective API.
  • Source/TargetApplicationInstances: The ids of the source and target application instances. The ids can be also retrieved via API. The ids given here are examples only and do not refer to any standard content.

By using this API multiple entities in the backend are created, the business solution request itself remains in state draft.

The API will also return the id of the generated interface request. We assume 

07d31b7a-eb9f-4091-89c7-37913cd7a392 for now.

Submit Business Solution Request

After the interface request was created and the questionnaire for the business solution request was answered (or deactivated), the request can be submitted.

PATCH on https://intas-api.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/intas/entities/v1/Request('8deb4b5c-0679-4048-ad9c-fd4956a0025c')

{
	"Status": "new"
}

see full specification here

The call to the API triggers the state change of the request and sets it open.

Process Interface Requests

After the request is submitted, the individual interface requests need to be processed and a technology must be selected. As mentioned earlier, this should be done using the Integration Assessment UI. With the ID of the previously generated interface requests, you can construct the URL to access the interface request by appending the id.

In our case, the URL is https://thisisanexample.intas.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/app/index.html#/Worklist/InterfaceAssessment/07d31b7a-eb9f-4091-89c7-37913cd7a392

By accessing this URL and starting to process the interface request, the related business solution request will change its status to in progress.

After the technology has been selected, you are also able to download a PDF report of the interface request. You may use this file and attach it to an external system to document the technology selection.

Complete Business Solution Request

After all interface requests have been processed, you are able to complete the request.

PATCH on https://intas-api.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/intas/entities/v1/Request('8deb4b5c-0679-4048-ad9c-fd4956a0025c')

{
	"Status": "completed"
}

see full specification here

The call to the API triggers the state change of the request and sets it to completed.

In case you want to remove the business solution request completely you can also delete the entity:

DELETE on https://intas-api.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/intas/entities/v1/Request('8deb4b5c-0679-4048-ad9c-fd4956a0025c')

see full specification here

Summary

This blog article has shown how to use the Integration Assessment APIs to manage the request workflow. By doing so, it is possible to integrate the Integration Assessment into external systems and workflows.

entry.title: Migration story of 48 SAP SuccessFactors HCM successful integrations in 4 Months
entry.content:
1. Purpose of the document:

This document details how we migrated 48 SAP SuccessFactors HCM Boomi integrations to SAP Integration Suite successfully in 4 Months. We explain the strategy, execution, and different types of integration migration. 

2. Why SAP SuccessFactors HCM customer needs to migrate from Boomi to SAP Integration Suite: 

  1. Between 2013 and 2016, SAP SuccessFactors HCM customers used BOOMI as an integration platform to support data integration requirements in SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation, and many customers are still running on Boomi
  2. SAP Customers have significantly invested in SAP BTP and SAP Integration Suite in the last seven years as part of S4HANA transitions and Integration Modernization (moving from SAP PO to SAP IS). 
  3. Customers are migrating interfaces from Boomi to the SAP Integration Suite, aligning with the SAP Integration strategy.
  4. Maintaining multiple integration platforms adds a lot of Technical Debt, and SAP customers consolidate and move to one integration platform (SAP Integration Suite) 

3. SAP SuccessFactors- Boomi Integration Landscape:

The customer implemented SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central in 2015 and utilized the BOOMI Integration Platform, building 48 integrations to support all Integration requirements.

 

 

4. Boomi to SAP Integration Suite Migration –  Framework:

Created a detailed migration framework to cover end to end execution 

 

 

Interface Mapping : Existing BOOMI interfaces evaluated  and mapped

 

 

5. Interface Development Approach Decision Process and Guidelines: 

 

 

  1. We implement prepackaged integrations developed by SAP to reduce development effort and customization. 
  2. We use SAP SuccessFactors Integration Center (IC) Templates for all simple to moderately complex Integrations.  
  3. We have built reusable assets to reuse . 
  4. Have used SF OData APIs. 
  5. Have checked with vendors about API integration capabilities to avoid the SFTP files approach. 
  6. Followed Real Time - Intelligent Services Based Event Driven approach wherever possible. 
  7. Strong governance in place and followed clean core capabilities and leveraged SAP IS standard capabilities. 
  8. Built reusable assets, and every consultant followed the same Integration standards. 
  9. Documented detailed technical specification document, created an integration design process and mapping document.

6. SFEC to SAP ERP Integrations Migration :

The customer was using custom Boomi integrations to replicate Emp and Org data, and manual file imports to update bank master data and cost center data.

  1. Implemented prepackaged integrations developed by SAP to integrate SAP SuccessFactors EC to SAP ERP – Employee and Org data
  2. Implemented Cost Center Prepackaged Integration to replicate Cost Center data from SAP ERP to SF EC
  3. Implemented Prepackaged Integration Template to replicate Bank Master Data from SAP ERP to SF EC

 

 

7. Instance Strategy and Interface Code Management :

 The customer had 3 BOOMI atoms, and we recommended that the customer get the 3 Integration Suite tenants and used the same.

  1. Have used SAP Cloud Transport Management to transport the code and implemented the governance process to make sure the the Integration Code will not transport without approval from the the business owner 

 

 

2. Cloud to On-Premise Connectivity

  • Customer did not use Boomi Local Atoms  - Hence, we didn’t go with Edge integration cell but used SAP Cloud Connector for SAP ERP integrations
  • Established connectivity with SFTP servers and used SSH connectivity option, and implemented Encryption
  • SAP IS IP ranges info shared with Vendors,, and they have whitelisted the same to have a a successful handshake

 

 

3. SAP SuccessFactors HCM - SAP Integration Suitelandscape after Migration

 

 

  1. Workforce Software Integrations migration – Implemented new prepackaged integration delivered by Workforce Software to replicate employee data from SF EC to WFS and used SAP Prepackaged integration to replicate Time and Absences data to EC Payroll
  2. ServiceNow Integrations – Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee and Contingents data using SAP IS – APIs approach
  3. Tableau Integration – Tableau is directly integrated with SF EC and imported employee and Org data – Direct Integration
  4. OC Tanner Integration - Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee Data to OC Tanner
  5. ComputerShare Integration - Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee Data to Computer Share
  6. SailPoint Integrations – SailPoint built a direct integration to integrate SAP SuccessFactors EC – SailPoint, and used the same replicate Employee, Org, and Foundation data and matched the same file layout as Boomi
  7. Qualtrics Integration - Implemented Prepackaged Integration templates to replicate Employee Data to Qualtrics
  8. Data Hub – Explored option to integrate Data Hub with SF EC directly APIs, but significant design changes and development required at Data Hub, hence built five master integrations to replicate Employee, Org, and Foundation data and matched the same file layout as Boomi
  9. Internal Systems Integrations – Generate the same file layouts as BOOMI but used  integration templates 

 8.  Key Considerations: 

 

 

  1. Engaging an SAP Integration Suite expert who is familiar with the Boomi integration platform is key.
  2. Migrating interfaces in a phased manner is a good idea
  3. Use this migration as an opportunity to reduce the tech debt and implement prepackaged integrations or Direct Integrations
  4. Creating good documentation is key, and delivering the requirement, technical design, and data mapping document is mandatory for each integration
  5. Make sure every part of the project follows SAP Integration Development standards and SF APIs leading practices
  6. Plan enough time to perform testing
  7. Build an internal team that can validate SI’s work
  8. Reduce the impact on vendors and make sure Boomi and SAP IS generate the same files

entry.title: How to Connect a Third-Party Application to SAP CPI Using APIs and Access Token – A GreytHR Example
entry.content:
👉   Introduction
In modern integration scenarios, many third-party applications expose APIs to exchange data. SAP
Integration Suite (CPI) allows us to consume these APIs easily, even when token-based authentication is
required.
In this blog, I’ll walk you through how I integrated the GreytHR HRMS application with SAP using API
endpoints and access tokens. We'll fetch employee master data using a secure, token-based flow.

👉Topics Covered
• What is an API First Approach?
• What are APIs?
• Types of APIs.
• using API How to connect third party Applications.

•  What is an API First Approach?
The API First Approach is an approach to software design that focuses on the API to create applications that can be easily connected to each other. API First creates ecosystems of applications that are modular, reusable, and extensible, just like Lego bricks.


An API First Approach means that your APIs are treated as first class citizens. Everything revolves around the end product being used by mobile devices and client applications. An API First approach involves developing APIs that are consistent and reusable. This is achieved by using an API description language.

                                       

The graphic shows an example of API First Approach. Information is provided in the following text.
Explanation:


API Provider :
An API provider provides an interface with technical features (No. 1). Symbolically, this is shown in this picture with the ball- or lollipop notation. This interface can be consumed by a service (No. 5). There presentation corresponds to the socket notation (No. 2). The service acts in the role of the API consumer.

API Consumer :
The API consumer requires an interface (No. 3). The representation corresponds to the socket notation (No. 4). In this case, the API provider is the service (No. 5). The presentation is again made with the ball-or lollipop notation.

Find More Information at:
Understanding the API-First Approach to Building Products.
It is not Cloud first or API first but Strategy first. API Management Strategy in Mult iCloud Environments | SAP Blogs.

• What are APIs?
API stands for Application Programming Interface. An API specifies the operations as well as inputs and outputs of a software component. It defines functionalities that are independent of their respective implementations, so that these implementations can vary without affecting the user of the API.


• Types of APIs.
In the literature, there are many overviews of different types of APIs. Below is a simple overview of the APIs that we must understand for this course.

The graphic shows an overview of APIs. Information is provided in the following text.
Here, four different APIs are defined under the superset of APIs, based on their use.
Databased APIs These are intended for file exchange between systems. Files can be, for example, configuration files.


Object-orientated APIs
These are used in object-oriented programming languages to define the communication of the classes with each other.
Remote APIs (No. 1)
This includes today's important web APIs, such as REST and SOAP APIs. REST itself is not a protocol but a software architectural style.
Messaging APIs (No. 2)
•  These are asynchronous APIs that send events based on events. These are used in Event Driven
architectures.


• using API How to connect Third party Applications.
I am trying to connect the GreytHR application to SAP CPI using APIs.
Step-1
✅ Step 1: Create API in GreytHR
• Log into your GreytHR portal
• Navigate to the API Management section
• Click Create API
• Provide:

• API Name
• Role access (select “All Roles”)

Click on Next Save the generated Username and Password

✅ Step 2: Store Credentials in CPI
• In SAP Integration Suite, go to:
• Monitoring → Manage Security → Security Material
• Create a User Credential
• Enter the username and password received from GREYTHR
• Name it: GREYTHR_CREDENTIALS

✅ Step 3: Create Access Token Flow in CPI
• Open your design workspace and create a new Integration Flow
• Use a Timer as the start event (for scheduled execution)
• Add a Request-Reply block
• Use an HTTP Adapter with:
• Method: POST
• URL: GreytHR’s Access Token Endpoint
• Authentication: use GREYTHR_CREDENTIALS

✅ Step 4: Convert JSON to XML (if necessary) and Store Access Token
If the response is in JSON format (which is common for most token APIs):
• Add a JSON to XML Converter immediately after the Request-Reply step
• This ensures the token response can be processed in SAP CPI (which primarily works with XML)
If the response is already in XML format, you can skip the converter step
• Add a Content Modifier after the token call
• Extract the ACCESS_TOKEN from the response JSON
• Store it as a property: ${header . ACCESS_TOKEN}

✅ Step 5: Call GreytHR API to Fetch Data
• Add another Request-Reply block
• HTTP Adapter configuration:
• Method: GET or POST
• URL: GREYTHR API endpoint for employee data
• Authorization: Bearer ${header. ACCESS_TOKEN}

Deploy the iFlow
• If successful, you’ll receive employee master data from GREYTHR!

 👉 Conclusion
• Using APIs and token-based authentication, SAP CPI can securely connect to third-party systems
like GreytHR. This approach is:
• Scalable
• Reusable
• Suitable for any modern integration scenario
The same logic can be extended to tools like Salesforce, Zoho, or OpenWeatherMap.

entry.title: Timer and Trigger based SAP CPI Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In this blog post, we’ll explore how to design a single CPI interface that will work with scheduled based and also on demand trigger based.

Business Scenario

In most SuccessFactors integrations, we often need to schedule interfaces based on a timer. These interfaces read messages from SuccessFactors using the last modified date timestamp and process the data to target applications. Once the data is sent to the target application, we update the last modified date and store it in a variable. During the next run, the interface reads data based on the timestamp and processes the records.

We design the interface to avoid updating the timestamp if any errors occur. For example:

  • If the target application is down or there are connection issues.
  • If mandatory data is missing for some cases.

This approach works well if the target application processes all records as a single message. However, there are situations where we need to read all employee data during a specific time interval and process each employee's data individually employee by employee. In the Splitter:

  • If we enable stop on exception it will halt all record processing.
  • If we do not enable stop on exception we might miss processing some records.

For example, if we apply a last modified date query to employment data and encounter an issue, we can correct it, and the next run will process the modified data.

In another case, if we navigate to emergency contact data from the employment portlet and apply the last modified date query on employment data, any error in the emergency contact data will prevent the record from being processed to the target. Even after updating the emergency contact, it will not process because the query's last modified date is based on employment and not on emergency contact. To handle this, we can create another integration process with a trigger-based approach in the same iFlow to query and retrieve the data, then process it from the local integration. With this trigger we can manually run the integration by passing the employee id.

Solution Architecture

 

 

SAP CPI Development 

 

In the above SAP CPI screenshot we have two integration Process

  1. Time Based Integration: This will execute based on the pre-defined time interval Steps are below explained
    • Content Modifier for Schedule Query: This one will be used to read time stamp and also scheduled query to pass in next step.
    • Local Integration Process: This one will be main local integration process, based on the query received, it will call SF and do mapping and send details to the target application.
    • Write Time stamp in the variable. This will store last modified date to process in next run.
    • Application Id, this is optional , used for additional monitoring to know from and last modified date.
    • Below is Externalized Content Modifier

 

  1. Trigger Based Integration: This will have HTTP sender and it is trigger based. An Ad-Hoc basic, team will call this end point. We can use this in some UI and give it to business to run themselves
    • Content Modifier for Trigger Query: This one will be used read parameters from trigger and form the query to next step.
    • Local Integration Process: This one will be main local integration process, based on the query received, it will call SF and do mapping and send details to the target application.
    • Additional logging.
    • Below is externalized query in Content Modifier 

 

 

Once iflow deployed it will looks like below

  • Trigger endpoint URL
  • Timer execution for schedule.

 

 

Testing

Schedule Run

In the Timer I have used Application message id to know the time interval.

Trigger Run

postman for trigger

Monitoring

 

Note in the Trigger content modifier I have used ${header.SapAuthenticatedUserName} to know who triggered iflow.

Conclusion

We can design SAP CPI ilfows to work with  both Timer based and also trigger based approaches. Depending on the requirements we can modify these as needed. Additionally,  we can provide a trigger endpoint to API or UI application for business team use.

SAP Integration Suite API Management SAP SuccessFactors Platform

entry.title: Using Self Signed certificates with SAP Custom Domain service for Integration Flow Authentication
entry.content:
The SAP Custom Domain service allows you to use your own custom domain (e.g., yourcompany.com) for applications hosted on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP), replacing the default SAP-provided URLs. It enhances branding, security (with TLS/SSL certificates), and user experience.

 In this blog we explain how to use SAP custom domain service with self-signed certificates for exposing integration flows deployed on to SAP Integration Suite runtime.

Later user can invoke the deployed integration flows endpoints with SAP Custom Domain Service endpoints with OpenSSL generated self-signed certificates as depicted in below diagram.

 

This blog we can breakdown in to below steps

  1. Domain Registered with Squarespace
  2. Azure DNS as the Authoritative DNS
  3. Using SAP Custom Domain Service configured with Registered Domain and SAAS Routes
  4. Generating Self-Signed certificates with OpenSSL and Add it to the Custom Domain Service Trust List.  
  5. Create Process Integration Runtime instance in BTP subaccount with service key based on Self-Signed certificate.
  6. Using SAP Integration Suite to create integration flows for testing which uses both Client Certificate approach and User Role approach.
  7. Invoke the integration flow endpoint with Custom Domain Service route and self signed certificates.

Domain Registered with Squarespace

We do a simple domain registration, which we may use later to be configured in SAP custom domain service for testing purpose.

Here I registered a domain called mayurpoc.org.

Azure DNS as the Authoritative DNS

Instead of using your domain registrar's default DNS servers, you delegate your domain's DNS resolution to Azure DNS.

Create an DNS Zone for the domain we registered in previous step and create a DNS Record, i.e.  CNAME record which maps a host alias subdomain to SAP Integration suite runtime Integration flow deployed endpoint host.   

Copy all the Azure DNS name servers from above portal and configure in the  Squarespace for the registered domain.

This means you update the nameservers (NS records) at your domain registrar to point to the Azure DNS name servers, this Azure DNS  provides advanced DNS features (like traffic routing policies, private DNS zones, etc.) that your registrar might not offer.

Using SAP Custom Domain Service configured with Registered Domain and SAAS Routes

Under both the reserved domains and custom domains add the domain we have registered in the first step.

Create SAAS route mapping, which maps the CNAME we created in Azure DNS records to the actual Integration Suite runtime Host URL.

Create CSR under server certificates in SAP Custom domain service, download it   and use the Certbot from letsencrypt to generate a server certificate for your custom domain.

certbot.exe certonly –standalone --preferred-challenges dns --csr C:\path\to\your\existing.csr

complete the DNS challenge when it asks to create DNS records in the Azure DNS Zone.

Upon successful validation of DNS records, certbot would generate the server certificate.

Upload the server certificate for the reserved domain and activate the same.

Generating Self-Signed certificates with OpenSSL and Add it Custom Domain Service Trust List.  

 Generating self-signed certificates with OpenSSL is a common task for development, testing, or internal-only applications where you don't need a certificate signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (CA).

This command generates both a private key and a self-signed certificate directly.

openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:2048 -nodes -keyout private.key -out certificate.crt -days 365 -subj "/CN=*.mayurpoc.org/O=SAP/OU=PM/L=Bengaluru/ST=Karnataka/C=IN"

create a TLS configuration in the SAP custom domain service and add this Self-Signed certificate to the Trust List.

This TLS configuration should be assigned to the server certificate activated for configured reserved domain.

Create Process Integration Runtime instance in BTP subaccount  with service key based on Self-Signed certificate.

Create Process integration runtime instance of integration-flow plan type. Create an service key of key type “External certificate”

Copy the Self-Signed CRT certificate content into External certificate and create key.

Using SAP Integration Suite to create integration flows for testing which uses both Client Certificate approach and User Role approach

First integration flow is based on User role and we intended to do simple authentication request from an external client , so this iflow gives back custom hardcoded response on HTTP GET request.

Second integration flow is based on Client certificate where we have to configure the SubjectDN and IssuerDN in the http sender channel.

Invoke the integration flow endpoint with Custom Domain Service route and self signed certificates

     Here I have used postman to invoke the deployed integration flows. I have converted OpenSSL self signed certificates to pfx format and imported it in postman settings.

   Invoked the integration flow endpoint for first use case, i.e. healthCheck Iflow configured with User Role. As you see invocation successful with Self-Signed certificate.

   Invoked the integration flow endpoint for second use case, i.e. healthCheck_ssl Iflow configured with Client Certificate. As you see invocation successful with Self-Signed certificate.
